english: night, by night, in the night time Dzl., W.: *tsan-la* (mtshan la); dei mtsán-mo (de'i mtshan mo) Dzl. in that night; tsan gaṅ (mtshan gang), tsan toγ-tág (mtshan thog thag), W. also *tsan-tse-ri'* (mtshan tse ri), the whole night; also adv., all night; mtsan-dkyil (mtshan dkyil), mtsan-gúṅ (mtshan gung), mtsan-pyéd (mtshan phyed) midnight; mtsan-stód (mtshan stod), mtsan-smád (mtshan smad) the first, the second half of the night; mtsan-stód-kyi rmí-lam (mtshan stod kyi rmi lam) a dream before midnight Med. — mtsan-dús (mtshan dus) night time. — mtsan-byi (mtshan byi) (W. *tsan-bí'* (mtshan bi)) bat. — tsan-šiṅ (mtshan shing) W. 1. chip of pine-wood, 2. pine-wood. 3. pine-tree. — mtsan-só byéd-pa (mtshan so byed pa) to keep watch during the night Sch.

1. intermediate space, interstice, border, boundary-line, rgya-gár daṅ bál-poi mtsáms-na (rgya gar dang bal po'i mtshams na), rgya-bál-gyi mtsáms-su (rgya bal gyi mtshams su) on the border between India and Nepal Glr.; mtsáms-kyi nags-kród (mtshams kyi nags khrod) boundary-forest Glr.; sa-mtsáms (sa mtshams) (vulgo san-tsám (san mtsham)) frontier of the country Glr.; dé-nas °doms lṅa-brgyái mtsáms-nas (de nas 'doms lnga brgya'i mtshams nas) at a distance of 500 fathoms from that place; bar-mtsams-na yod (bar mtshams na yod) it lies in the middle between; rí taṅ mtsáms-su (ri thang mtshams su) where the mountains are contiguous to the plain; byaṅ šar mtsáms-su (byang shar mtshams su) in the north-east (cf. no. 2 below); ču °gram mtsáms-su (chu 'gram mtshams su) (between the water and the river's bank) close to the edge Wdn.; dei mtsáms-su (de'i mtshams su) (with regard to a royal dynasty) intervening, a usurper, interrupting the regular succession Glr.; cès-pai tsig mtsáms-nas (ces pa'i tshig mtshams nas) when these words were uttered, at these words Tar. 127, 11; sgo(i)-mtsáms (sgo'i mtshams) a narrow opening of the door, sgo-mtsáms-nas sleb (sgo mtshams nas sleb) (he or it) enters through the cleft of a door, equivalent to our 'through the key-hole'; *tsám-la čúg-cè* (mtsham la bcug ce) W. to preserve, to put (plants) between (paper), to pack up (glass in straw). — mtsams sbyor-ba (mtshams sbyor ba) 1. to close interstices, to stitch up, to sew together (the separate parts of a shoe) Mil. 2. Sch.: to occupy a certain space, to enter a womb', to embody one's self in human flesh, so it seems to be used in Thgr. and Mil. 3. to take a resolution, to form a plan, to conceive an idea, to settle in one's mind, like °god-pa ('god pa), cf. sbyór-ba (sbyor ba) I, 2; II, 2 C., W. — mtsams °byé-ba (mtshams 'bye ba) to split(?), skra smin γsár-du mtsams-bye rtsub kyil (skra smin gsar du mtshams bye rtsub 'khyil) S.g. the hair of the head and the eye-brows splits, divides again, is growing thin, crisp, and interspersed with bald places, which is alledged to be a symptom of approaching death, yet hardly founded on correct observation, nor by any means clearly defined; Schr. has: skra mtsams byéd-pa (skra mtshams byed pa) to part the hair on the top of the head. — mtsams-med-pa (mtshams med pa) 1. adj., Ssk. ānantarya, without interstices, continuous, = go-mtsams-med-pa (go mtshams med pa) v. go (go) 1, Dzl. 2. sbst., Ssk. ānantarya, Was. (240), 'where nothing is to be interposed between a deed and its consequences, where the consequences are not to be averted', a deadly, capital sin Dzl. and elsewh.; mtsáms-med-pa lṅa (mtshams med pa lnga), i. e. inexpiable sins, are: parricide and matricide, murder of an Arhat (dgrá-bčom-pa) (dgra bcom pa), or of a Tathāgata, likewise causing divisions among the priesthood. — dus-mtsáms (dus mtshams) intermediate time Cs. — mtsams-sbyór (mtshams sbyor) the Sanskrit diphthongs ē, ō, ai, au; mtsams-sbyór-pa (mtshams sbyor pa) and -ma (-ma), a bawd, Cs. — mtsams(-kyi)-žu(-ba) (mtshams kyi zhu ba), also °tsams-žu ('tshams zhu), an expression gen. occurring in modern Tibetan letters, winding up the complimentary phrases of the introduction, and passing over to the proper business of the letter; for the immediate sense of the phrase I found no explanation. — 2. the points of the compass, mtsams bži (mtshams bzhi) the four cardinal points of the horizon; mtsams brgyad (mtshams brgyad) includes the intermediate points, south-east etc., mtsams drug (mtshams drug) denotes the four cardinal points together with the zenith and nadir. — 3. demarcation, partition, break, pause, stop, mtsams γčod-pa (mtshams gcod pa) to make a stop or pause with the voice in reading Gram.; esp. to draw a line of demarcation about one's own person, whether it be by a magic circle (Dom.), or by retiring to a solitary house, either for the sake of private study (Zam.), or which is most frq. the case, for religious meditation, (*tsám-la dád-cè* (mtsham la bsdad ce) W.) in the cell of a cloister, or in a hermitage or cave in the mountains, the seclusion lasting sometimes for several months, during which time the scanty food is silently received from without through a small aperture.
